reviewed.by: quote-wall reel: the church fathers on the deity of Christ, pre-Nicaea. Grounded in library/early_church_trinity.html (orthodoxy-CLEAN). GATES: orthodoxy CLEAN (9/9 scenes, 0 flags); argument SOUND (0 BREAK/WEAK); citations verified 4/5 quotes verbatim + all dates/attributions, with 1 CHECK APPLIED — Ignatius 'Jesus Christ our God' (Eph. salutation, Roberts-Donaldson), Pliny 'a hymn to Christ, as to a god' (Letters 10.96), Irenaeus 'our Lord, and God, and Saviour, and King' (Against Heresies 1.10.1), Clement 'he alone being both, both God and man' (Protrepticus 1) all confirmed verbatim; the Tertullian line was DE-QUOTED (his actual Latin is una substantia/tres personae, so 'three persons, one substance' is now framed as his Latin FORMULA, not a verbatim quote, and scene 2 changed 'Real quotes' -> 'Their own words'). Positive case only. Omits Melito (anti-Jewish-pull-quote risk) and Justin (subordinationist language). Claims early confession of deity, not the later homoousios metaphysics.

voiceover: Did the early church believe Jesus was God? Don't take my word for it — take theirs, in their own writing, more than a century before the Council of Nicaea. Around the year 107, Ignatius of Antioch — a bishop on his way to be executed — wrote to a church of 'Jesus Christ our God.' Around 112, even a hostile Roman governor, Pliny, reported that Christians gathered before dawn to sing 'a hymn to Christ, as to a god.' Around 180, Irenaeus of Lyons hailed him as 'our Lord, and God, and Saviour, and King.' Around 195, Clement of Alexandria wrote that the Word has now appeared as man — 'he alone being both, both God and man.' And around 210, Tertullian of Carthage gave it its Latin formula — three persons, one substance — and coined the very word: Trinity. Every one of those quotes was written before the Council of Nicaea in the year 325. The church did not invent the divinity of Jesus at a council. They inherited it — and they wrote it down. The words were there all along.

sub.t: The church fathers, in their own words.

lines.t: He gave it its Latin formula —

sub.t: The words were there all along.
